<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Quantum groups</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\operatorname{Spec}',
        '\\Gal':'\\operatorname{Gal}',
        '\\Hom':'\\operatorname{Hom}',
        '\\tr':'\\operatorname{tr}',
        '\\ad':'\\operatorname{ad}',
        '\\ind':'\\operatorname{ind}'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

← Notebook
1 Hopf algebras 2 $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ 3 $R$-matrix 4 Representations 5 Knot invariants 6 Applications

Quantum groups

Deform the universal enveloping algebra $U(\mathfrak g)$ by a single parameter $q$ and the cocommutativity of group multiplication breaks: tensor products of representations no longer commute, but they almost do, witnessed by an $R$-matrix that satisfies the Yang–Baxter equation. The shadow of that single failure of symmetry runs through knot polynomials, integrable lattice models, and categorification.

blocks.html:
1. Hopf algebras

A Hopf algebra $(H,m,\eta,\Delta,\varepsilon,S)$ over a field $k$ has five structure maps:

MapTypeRole
$m\colon H\otimes H\to H$multiplicationassociative
$\eta\colon k\to H$unit$\eta(1)=1_H$
$\Delta\colon H\to H\otimes H$comultiplicationcoassociative
$\varepsilon\colon H\to k$counit$(\varepsilon\otimes\mathrm{id})\Delta=(\mathrm{id}\otimes\varepsilon)\Delta=\mathrm{id}$
$S\colon H\to H$antipodeconvolution-inverse of $\mathrm{id}$

Bialgebra axioms force $\Delta$ and $\varepsilon$ to be algebra homomorphisms. The antipode is the unique map satisfying $m\circ(S\otimes\mathrm{id})\circ\Delta = m\circ(\mathrm{id}\otimes S)\circ\Delta = \eta\circ\varepsilon$ — by abstract nonsense this forces $S$ to be an algebra anti-homomorphism.

Two prototypes

For a finite group $G$, the group algebra $\mathbb C[G]$ is a Hopf algebra with

$$\Delta(g)=g\otimes g,\qquad \varepsilon(g)=1,\qquad S(g)=g^{-1}\quad (g\in G).$$

It is cocommutative ($\tau\circ\Delta=\Delta$, where $\tau$ is the flip). For a Lie algebra $\mathfrak g$, the universal enveloping algebra $U(\mathfrak g)$ is also Hopf with

$$\Delta(x)=x\otimes 1+1\otimes x,\qquad \varepsilon(x)=0,\qquad S(x)=-x\quad (x\in\mathfrak g),$$

extended as algebra maps. Both are cocommutative — quantization will break this.

Hopf axiom inspector
pick an axiom; see the commutative square it asserts

blocks.html:
A commutative algebra of functions on a group $G$ ($\mathbb C[G]$ if $G$ is finite) is dual to the cocommutative $\mathbb C[G]$: the Hopf structure is a single algebraic object that simultaneously sees the algebra of functions and the group operation. Quantum groups are the deformations where neither side is commutative anymore.

  • Tensor products and dual representations

</section>

2. Drinfeld–Jimbo $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$

Fix a nonzero $q\in\mathbb C$ with $q\ne\pm 1$. The quantized enveloping algebra $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ is generated by $E$, $F$, $K$, $K^{-1}$ subject to

$$KK^{-1}=K^{-1}K=1,\qquad KEK^{-1}=q^2 E,\qquad KFK^{-1}=q^{-2} F,$$

$$[E,F] \;=\; \dfrac{K-K^{-1}}{q-q^{-1}}.$$

The Hopf structure is determined by

$$\Delta(K)=K\otimes K,\qquad \Delta(E)=E\otimes 1+K\otimes E,\qquad \Delta(F)=F\otimes K^{-1}+1\otimes F,$$

$$S(K)=K^{-1},\quad S(E)=-K^{-1}E,\quad S(F)=-FK,\quad \varepsilon(K)=1,\;\varepsilon(E)=\varepsilon(F)=0.$$

Why this is a deformation

Set $K=q^H$ formally and expand in $\hbar=\log q$:

$$KEK^{-1}=q^2 E \;\Rightarrow\; (1+\hbar H+\cdots)E(1-\hbar H+\cdots) = (1+2\hbar+\cdots)E,$$

which to first order is $[H,E]=2E$. Similarly $[E,F] = (K-K^{-1})/(q-q^{-1}) \to H$ in the limit. So $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)\rightsquigarrow U(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ as $q\to 1$ — a smooth deformation of the classical relations.

$q$-deformation slider
watch $[E,F]$ interpolate between the quantum and classical bracket
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.querySelector("#qsl2-svg");
  const out = document.querySelector("#qsl2-out");
  const slider = document.querySelector("#q-slider");
  const qval = document.querySelector("#q-val");
  if(!svg || !out || !slider) return;
  const W = 700, H = 220;
  const x0 = 60, x1 = W - 30;
  const y0 = 30, y1 = H - 40;
  function plot(q){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:y1,x2:x1,y2:y1,stroke:"var(--line)","stroke-width":1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:y0,x2:x0,y2:y1,stroke:"var(--line)","stroke-width":1}));
    // baseline H = log eigenvalue (n=1..6)
    const ns = [1,2,3,4,5,6];
    const xs = ns.map((n,i) => x0 + (i+0.5)*((x1-x0)/ns.length));
    // classical: [E,F] eigenvalue on a weight-n vector is n
    // quantum: [n]_q = (q^n - q^(-n))/(q - q^(-1))
    const qbr = (n) => {
      if(Math.abs(q-1) < 1e-6) return n;
      return (Math.pow(q,n) - Math.pow(q,-n)) / (q - 1/q);
    };
    const classicals = ns;
    const quantums = ns.map(qbr);
    const allVals = [...classicals, ...quantums];
    const ymax = Math.max(...allVals.map(Math.abs)) * 1.2 + 0.5;
    const ymin = -1;
    function yOf(v){ return y1 - (v - ymin) / (ymax - ymin) * (y1 - y0); }
    // x-axis labels
    ns.forEach((n,i) => {
      const t = SVG("text",{x:xs[i],y:y1+18,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t.textContent = "n="+n;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    // y-axis label
    const ylab = SVG("text",{x:30,y:y0+12,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    ylab.textContent = "eigval";
    svg.appendChild(ylab);
    // bars: classical (yellow) and quantum (cyan)
    const barW = (x1-x0)/ns.length * 0.32;
    classicals.forEach((v,i) => {
      const yc = yOf(v);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:xs[i]-barW-2, y:Math.min(yc,yOf(0)), width:barW, height:Math.abs(yc-yOf(0)),
        fill:"var(--yellow)", opacity:0.8}));
    });
    quantums.forEach((v,i) => {
      const yc = yOf(v);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:xs[i]+2, y:Math.min(yc,yOf(0)), width:barW, height:Math.abs(yc-yOf(0)),
        fill:"var(--cyan)", opacity:0.8}));
    });
    // legend
    const lg = SVG("g",{transform:"translate(" + (x1-180) + ", " + (y0+10) + ")"});
    lg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:0,width:14,height:10,fill:"var(--yellow)"}));
    const t1 = SVG("text",{x:20,y:9,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    t1.textContent = "classical n";
    lg.appendChild(t1);
    lg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:18,width:14,height:10,fill:"var(--cyan)"}));
    const t2 = SVG("text",{x:20,y:27,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    t2.textContent = "quantum [n]_q";
    lg.appendChild(t2);
    svg.appendChild(lg);
    // axes baseline at zero
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:yOf(0),x2:x1,y2:yOf(0),stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":0.7,"stroke-dasharray":"3 3"}));
  }
  function update(){
    const q = parseFloat(slider.value);
    qval.textContent = "q = " + q.toFixed(2);
    plot(q);
    const dist = Math.abs(q - 1);
    let msg = "[E,F] acts on the weight-n vector by [n]_q = (q^n-q^(-n))/(q-q^(-1));";
    if(dist < 0.02) msg += "\nat q=1 this collapses to n — the classical eigenvalue of H.";
    else msg += `\nat q=${q.toFixed(2)} the spacing q-deforms but stays positive integer at n=1.`;
    out.textContent = msg;
  }
  slider.addEventListener("input", update);
  update();
})();
</script>

3. The universal $R$-matrix and Yang–Baxter

$U_q(\mathfrak g)$ is quasitriangular: there is an invertible element $R\in U_q(\mathfrak g)\widehat\otimes U_q(\mathfrak g)$ — the universal $R$-matrix — that intertwines the comultiplication with its opposite,

$$\Delta^{op}(x) \;=\; R\,\Delta(x)\,R^{-1}\qquad\text{for all }x\in U_q(\mathfrak g),$$

and satisfies the Yang–Baxter equation

$$R_{12}\,R_{13}\,R_{23} \;=\; R_{23}\,R_{13}\,R_{12} \quad\text{in } U_q(\mathfrak g)^{\otimes 3}.$$

Here $R_{ij}$ is $R$ inserted in the $i$-th and $j$-th tensor slots and identity elsewhere. For $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ on $V_1\otimes V_1$ (with $V_1$ the 2-dimensional standard rep) the matrix in the basis $\{v\otimes v, v\otimes w, w\otimes v, w\otimes w\}$ reads

$$R \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} q & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & q-q^{-1} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & q \end{pmatrix}.$$

Geometric meaning: the third Reidemeister move

Read each $R_{ij}$ as a positive crossing of strands $i$ and $j$. The Yang–Baxter relation is then exactly Reidemeister III, the move that allows sliding a strand across a crossing. So $R$ furnishes a representation of the braid group $B_n$ on $V^{\otimes n}$.

Yang–Baxter as Reidemeister III
flip the side: both sides equal $R_{12}R_{13}R_{23} = R_{23}R_{13}R_{12}$
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.querySelector("#ybe-svg");
  const out = document.querySelector("#ybe-out");
  const bL = document.querySelector("#ybe-left");
  const bR = document.querySelector("#ybe-right");
  if(!svg || !out) return;
  const W = 600, H = 260;
  const lanes = [120, 300, 480];
  const colors = ["var(--cyan)","var(--yellow)","var(--pink)"];
  // crossing helper: strand a goes over strand b at vertical center y
  function crossing(svg, xa, xb, y, h, over, color1, color2){
    // strand from (xa, y - h/2) -> (xb, y + h/2): goes over if `over`
    // strand from (xb, y - h/2) -> (xa, y + h/2): goes under if `over`
    const ya = y - h/2, yb = y + h/2;
    const drawStrand = (x1,x2,col,under) => {
      const path = SVG("path",{
        d: `M${x1} ${ya} C ${x1} ${y}, ${x2} ${y}, ${x2} ${yb}`,
        fill:"none", stroke: col, "stroke-width":4, "stroke-linecap":"round"
      });
      if(under){
        // gap effect: draw a thicker bg first
        const bg = SVG("path",{
          d: `M${x1} ${ya} C ${x1} ${y}, ${x2} ${y}, ${x2} ${yb}`,
          fill:"none", stroke: "var(--panel)", "stroke-width":10
        });
        svg.appendChild(bg);
      }
      svg.appendChild(path);
    };
    // first draw the under strand fully, then over with gap
    if(over){
      // strand b -> a is over; strand a -> b is under
      drawStrand(xa, xb, color1, true);
      drawStrand(xb, xa, color2, false);
    } else {
      drawStrand(xb, xa, color2, true);
      drawStrand(xa, xb, color1, false);
    }
  }
  function straight(svg, x, y1, y2, color){
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x,y1:y1,x2:x,y2:y2,stroke:color,"stroke-width":4,"stroke-linecap":"round"}));
  }
  function render(side){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    // top labels
    [0,1,2].forEach(i => {
      const t = SVG("text",{x:lanes[i],y:18,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t.textContent = "V"+(i+1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    // three crossings stacked vertically
    // left side: R12 (between lane 0 and 1), R13 (between lane 0 and 2), R23 (between lane 1 and 2)
    // right side: R23, R13, R12
    const yTop = 40, h = 60;
    let crossings;
    if(side === "L"){
      crossings = [
        {a:0,b:1, y:yTop+h/2, label:"R₁₂"},
        {a:0,b:2, y:yTop+h+h/2, label:"R₁₃"},
        {a:1,b:2, y:yTop+2*h+h/2, label:"R₂₃"}
      ];
    } else {
      crossings = [
        {a:1,b:2, y:yTop+h/2, label:"R₂₃"},
        {a:0,b:2, y:yTop+h+h/2, label:"R₁₃"},
        {a:0,b:1, y:yTop+2*h+h/2, label:"R₁₂"}
      ];
    }
    // We need to track strand color through the diagram.  We'll permute colors
    // at each crossing.  Initial strand colors:
    let strands = [colors[0], colors[1], colors[2]];
    // Draw straight passing strands above the first crossing
    for(let i=0;i<3;i++){
      straight(svg, lanes[i], yTop, yTop+h-1, strands[i]);
    }
    // Wait — instead, render each crossing as a self-contained box with three lanes.
    // We split each crossing into a box of height h centered at .y.
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    // re-render labels
    [0,1,2].forEach(i => {
      const t = SVG("text",{x:lanes[i],y:18,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t.textContent = "V"+(i+1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    let curColors = [colors[0], colors[1], colors[2]];
    for(let k=0;k<crossings.length;k++){
      const c = crossings[k];
      const yMid = c.y;
      const yA = yMid - h/2, yB = yMid + h/2;
      // identify which lanes are crossing and which is straight
      const passLanes = [0,1,2].filter(i => i !== c.a && i !== c.b);
      // draw straight strands
      passLanes.forEach(i => straight(svg, lanes[i], yA, yB, curColors[i]));
      // draw crossing on c.a / c.b
      crossing(svg, lanes[c.a], lanes[c.b], yMid, h, true, curColors[c.a], curColors[c.b]);
      // swap colors
      const tmp = curColors[c.a];
      curColors[c.a] = curColors[c.b];
      curColors[c.b] = tmp;
      // label on the right
      const lab = SVG("text",{x:W-30,y:yMid+4,"text-anchor":"end","font-size":13,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      lab.textContent = c.label;
      svg.appendChild(lab);
    }
    // bottom labels (final permutation)
    [0,1,2].forEach(i => {
      const t = SVG("text",{x:lanes[i],y:H-8,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t.textContent = (curColors[i] === colors[0]) ? "V1" : (curColors[i] === colors[1]) ? "V2" : "V3";
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    out.textContent = (side === "L"
      ? "Left side: R12 then R13 then R23 — strand 1 weaves first."
      : "Right side: R23 then R13 then R12 — strand 3 weaves first.")
      + "\nBoth produce the permutation (1 2 3) → (3 2 1) and equal operators.  Yang–Baxter says the two stacks of crossings are isotopic.";
  }
  bL.addEventListener("click", () => { bL.classList.add("active"); bR.classList.remove("active"); render("L"); });
  bR.addEventListener("click", () => { bR.classList.add("active"); bL.classList.remove("active"); render("R"); });
  render("L");
})();
</script>

blocks.html: When $q=1$ the formula $R = q\,P_+ + q^{-1}P_- + (q-q^{-1})\,(\text{off-diag})$ collapses to the identity flip — symmetric monoidal, no braiding. The $R$-matrix is exactly the obstruction to symmetric, and that obstruction is what makes knot invariants nontrivial.

Used in
Quantum groups · Reshetikhin–Turaev knot invariants
</section>

4. Representations and crystal bases

Over generic $q$ (i.e. $q$ not a root of unity), finite-dimensional type-1 $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$-modules look classical: the irreducibles $V_n$ for $n\in\mathbb Z_{\ge 0}$ have dimension $n+1$ with weight basis $\{v_n, v_{n-2}, \dots, v_{-n}\}$, where

$$Kv_k = q^k v_k,\qquad Ev_k = [\,(n-k)/2 + 1\,]_q\, v_{k+2},\qquad Fv_k = [\,(n+k)/2\,]_q\, v_{k-2},$$

and $[m]_q = (q^m - q^{-m})/(q-q^{-1})$ is the $q$-integer. Tensor products decompose by a quantum Clebsch–Gordan rule

$$V_m \otimes V_n \;\cong\; \bigoplus_{k=0}^{\min(m,n)} V_{\,m+n-2k}.$$

Crystals — the $q\to 0$ shadow

Kashiwara's crystal basis $B(V_n)$ for $V_n$ is the set $\{v_n, v_{n-2}, \dots, v_{-n}\}$ regarded as the vertices of a coloured graph with one $F$-arrow between consecutive weight vectors. Tensor products of crystals are computed by an explicit combinatorial rule on pairs of arrow strings: irreducible components match irreducible factors of the quantum tensor product.

Crystal-graph tensor product for $\mathfrak{sl}_2$
pick highest weights — see $V_m\otimes V_n$ decompose into a sum of crystals

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.querySelector("#crystal-svg");
  const out = document.querySelector("#crystal-out");
  const inM = document.querySelector("#cr-m");
  const inN = document.querySelector("#cr-n");
  if(!svg || !out) return;
  function render(){
    const m = Math.max(1, Math.min(5, parseInt(inM.value)||2));
    const n = Math.max(1, Math.min(5, parseInt(inN.value)||3));
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    // decomposition: V_{m+n}, V_{m+n-2}, ..., V_{|m-n|}
    const components = [];
    for(let k=Math.abs(m-n); k<=m+n; k+=2) components.push(k);
    components.reverse(); // largest first
    // layout: each component as a horizontal row of dots with arrows
    const padX = 60, rowH = 40;
    const yStart = 30;
    const maxNodes = m+n+1;
    const dx = (700 - 2*padX) / maxNodes;
    components.forEach((d, i) => {
      const y = yStart + i*rowH;
      const numNodes = d+1;
      const xStart = padX + ((maxNodes - numNodes)/2) * dx;
      // arrows
      for(let j=0;j<numNodes-1;j++){
        const x1 = xStart + j*dx;
        const x2 = xStart + (j+1)*dx;
        drawArrow(svg, [x1,y], [x2,y], {color:"var(--cyan)", markerId:"cr-"+i+"-"+j, pad1:8, pad2:10});
      }
      // nodes
      for(let j=0;j<numNodes;j++){
        const x = xStart + j*dx;
        svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:x,cy:y,r:6,fill:"var(--yellow)",stroke:"var(--ink)","stroke-width":1}));
      }
      // label on left
      const lab = SVG("text",{x:padX-10, y:y+4, "text-anchor":"end","font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)"});
      lab.textContent = "V_" + d;
      svg.appendChild(lab);
    });
    out.textContent = `V_${m} ⊗ V_${n}  =  ${components.map(d => "V_"+d).join("  ⊕  ")}\n` +
                      `dimensions: ${(m+1)}·${(n+1)} = ${(m+1)*(n+1)} = ` +
                      components.map(d => d+1).join(" + ");
  }
  inM.addEventListener("input", render);
  inN.addEventListener("input", render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Lusztig's canonical basis

Lusztig constructed a distinguished $\mathbb Z[q,q^{-1}]$-basis $\mathbf B$ of the integral form of $U_q^-(\mathfrak g)$ that is

  • bar-invariant: $\overline q = q^{-1}$ fixes each basis element,
  • has positive integer structure constants for multiplication and the bar involution,
  • compatible with all highest-weight modules: each $V_\lambda$ has a canonical basis subset of $\mathbf B$.

The positivity of these structure constants is a deep theorem; it was an early hint that there should be a categorification — vector-space dimensions whose Euler characteristic is the basis element. KLR algebras supply exactly such a categorification.

See also
  • Lie algebras — Cartan, Killing, semisimplicity
  • Representation theory — group representations

Used in
Quantum groups · Reshetikhin–Turaev knot invariants
</section>

5. Reshetikhin–Turaev knot invariants

A framed oriented tangle is a 1-manifold with boundary in $\mathbb R^2\times[0,1]$ whose endpoints lie on $\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}$. Reshetikhin and Turaev built a tensor functor

$$F\colon \mathbf{Tang}_V \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{Rep}\bigl(U_q(\mathfrak g)\bigr)$$

from the category of $V$-labelled tangles to $U_q(\mathfrak g)$-modules. The functor is forced by three pieces of data:

  • Crossing $\mapsto$ braiding $c_{V,V} = \tau\circ R_{V,V}$,
  • Cup / cap $\mapsto$ evaluation / coevaluation on $V\otimes V^*$ (rigid duality),
  • Framing twist $\mapsto$ the ribbon element $\theta$.

For an oriented knot $K$ closed up to a 1-tangle from $\mathbb C\to\mathbb C$, the functor returns a scalar — the quantum knot invariant:

$$J_K(q) \;=\; F(K) \;\in\; \mathbb Z[q^{\pm 1/2}].$$

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.querySelector("#rt-svg");
  const out = document.querySelector("#rt-out");
  if(!svg || !out) return;
  const buttons = document.querySelectorAll("#rt-vis button[data-knot]");
  function arc(svg, x, y, r, start, end, color, ccw=false){
    // draw arc from angle start to angle end (radians), centered at (x,y)
    const x1 = x + r*Math.cos(start), y1 = y + r*Math.sin(start);
    const x2 = x + r*Math.cos(end), y2 = y + r*Math.sin(end);
    let large = (end - start) > Math.PI ? 1 : 0;
    let sweep = ccw ? 0 : 1;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:`M${x1} ${y1} A ${r} ${r} 0 ${large} ${sweep} ${x2} ${y2}`,
      fill:"none",stroke:color,"stroke-width":4,"stroke-linecap":"round"}));
  }
  function strand(svg, x1, y1, x2, y2, color, withGap=false){
    if(withGap){
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1,y1,x2,y2,stroke:"var(--panel)","stroke-width":10}));
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1,y1,x2,y2,stroke:color,"stroke-width":4,"stroke-linecap":"round"}));
  }
  function curve(svg, x1, y1, cx1, cy1, cx2, cy2, x2, y2, color, withGap=false){
    if(withGap){
      svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:`M${x1} ${y1} C ${cx1} ${cy1}, ${cx2} ${cy2}, ${x2} ${y2}`,
        fill:"none", stroke:"var(--panel)","stroke-width":10}));
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:`M${x1} ${y1} C ${cx1} ${cy1}, ${cx2} ${cy2}, ${x2} ${y2}`,
      fill:"none", stroke:color, "stroke-width":4, "stroke-linecap":"round"}));
  }
  function render(knot){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    const cyan = "var(--cyan)", yel = "var(--yellow)", pink = "var(--pink)";
    if(knot === "unknot"){
      // simple ellipse
      svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx:300,cy:140,rx:120,ry:90,fill:"none",stroke:cyan,"stroke-width":4}));
      out.textContent = "Unknot.  No crossings ⇒ R-matrix never appears.\nF(unknot) = qdim(V) = [n+1]_q for V = V_n.";
      return;
    }
    if(knot === "hopf"){
      // two interlocking ellipses
      svg.appendChild(SVG("ellipse",{cx:230,cy:140,rx:100,ry:70,fill:"none",stroke:cyan,"stroke-width":4}));
      // second ellipse with two arcs to fake under-over
      // top arc
      svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:"M 240 140 A 100 70 0 0 1 440 140",fill:"none",stroke:yel,"stroke-width":4}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:"M 240 140 A 100 70 0 0 0 440 140",fill:"none",stroke:"var(--panel)","stroke-width":10}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d:"M 240 140 A 100 70 0 0 0 440 140",fill:"none",stroke:yel,"stroke-width":4}));
      // labels
      const t1 = SVG("text",{x:230,y:30,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t1.textContent = "component A"; svg.appendChild(t1);
      const t2 = SVG("text",{x:440,y:30,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t2.textContent = "component B"; svg.appendChild(t2);
      // crossing labels
      const r1 = SVG("text",{x:250,y:150,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--cyan)","font-style":"italic"});
      r1.textContent = "R"; svg.appendChild(r1);
      const r2 = SVG("text",{x:420,y:150,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--cyan)","font-style":"italic"});
      r2.textContent = "R"; svg.appendChild(r2);
      out.textContent = "Hopf link: 2 positive crossings.\nF(Hopf) = (qtr ⊗ qtr)(R₂₁ R₁₂)\nFor V_1 of U_q(sl_2): F(Hopf) = q² + q⁻²  (Jones up to normalization).";
      return;
    }
    if(knot === "trefoil"){
      // stylized trefoil: three lobes around a center
      const cx = 300, cy = 150;
      const R = 90;
      // three lobes at angles 90°, 210°, 330° (in degrees)
      // simpler: draw three crossings around the center with arcs
      // Use a parametric trefoil: x = sin(t) + 2 sin(2t), y = cos(t) - 2 cos(2t)
      let pts = [];
      const N = 200;
      for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
        const t = i/N * Math.PI * 2;
        const x = cx + 30 * (Math.sin(t) + 2*Math.sin(2*t));
        const y = cy + 30 * (Math.cos(t) - 2*Math.cos(2*t));
        pts.push([x,y]);
      }
      // draw under-arcs first (background gap), then over-arcs.  Simpler: just draw the curve as one path.
      let d = "M" + pts[0][0] + " " + pts[0][1];
      for(let i=1;i<pts.length;i++) d += " L " + pts[i][0] + " " + pts[i][1];
      svg.appendChild(SVG("path",{d,fill:"none",stroke:cyan,"stroke-width":4,"stroke-linecap":"round"}));
      // mark three crossings
      [0, Math.PI*2/3, Math.PI*4/3].forEach((th,i) => {
        const xx = cx + 60*Math.cos(th + Math.PI/2);
        const yy = cy + 60*Math.sin(th + Math.PI/2);
        svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:xx,cy:yy,r:14,fill:"none",stroke:yel,"stroke-width":1.5,"stroke-dasharray":"3 2"}));
        const t = SVG("text",{x:xx,y:yy+4,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:yel,"font-style":"italic"});
        t.textContent = "R";
        svg.appendChild(t);
      });
      out.textContent = "Trefoil 3_1: 3 positive crossings ⇒ each contributes one R.\nF(3_1) = qtr( c_{V,V} c_{V,V} c_{V,V} )\nJones polynomial: V(3_1) = -q⁻⁴ + q⁻³ + q⁻¹ (sign / normalization conventions vary).";
    }
  }
  buttons.forEach(b => b.addEventListener("click", () => {
    buttons.forEach(x => x.classList.remove("active"));
    b.classList.add("active");
    render(b.dataset.knot);
  }));
  render("hopf");
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Witten subsequently realized the Jones polynomial as an expectation value of a Wilson loop in 3d Chern–Simons gauge theory at level $k$ with $q=e^{2\pi i/(k+2)}$ — a path-integral derivation of the same algebraic invariant. Reshetikhin–Turaev is the rigorous, finite-dimensional shadow of that quantum field theory.

Used in
Quantum groups · Affine, KZ, and categorification
</section>

6. Affine quantum groups, KZ, and categorification

The Drinfeld–Jimbo construction extends to the affine Lie algebras $\widehat{\mathfrak g}$, giving $U_q(\widehat{\mathfrak g})$. Its representations sit on top of an evaluation map $V(z) = V\otimes\mathbb C[z,z^{-1}]$ and the spectral $R$-matrix $R(z/w)$ depends on the ratio of evaluation parameters.

Integrable lattice models

For the six-vertex model, the Boltzmann weights of a vertex configuration are exactly the entries of the spectral $R$-matrix of $U_q(\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}_2})$ on $V_1\otimes V_1$. Yang–Baxter $R_{12}(u)R_{13}(u+v)R_{23}(v) = R_{23}(v)R_{13}(u+v)R_{12}(u)$ implies that transfer matrices at different spectral parameters commute, which is the algebraic source of integrability.

Drinfeld–Kohno: KZ monodromy

The Knizhnik–Zamolodchikov flat connection on $\mathrm{Conf}_n(\mathbb C)$ with values in $\mathfrak g^{\otimes n}$ is

$$\nabla \;=\; d \;-\; \hbar\sum_{i

where $\Omega = \sum_a x_a\otimes x^a$ is the Casimir element. Drinfeld–Kohno: the monodromy representation of the braid group $B_n$ on $\bigl(V_1\otimes\cdots\otimes V_n\bigr)$ is canonically equivalent to the representation defined by the $R$-matrix of $U_q(\mathfrak g)$ at $q = e^{i\pi\hbar}$. Differential geometry on configuration space matches the algebra of quantum groups.

Categorification

Khovanov–Lauda–Rouquier (KLR) constructed a graded 2-category $\mathcal U$ whose Grothendieck group is $U_q(\mathfrak g)_{\mathbb Z[q,q^{-1}]}$. Each $q^k$ becomes a grading shift; multiplication becomes composition of 1-morphisms; canonical-basis elements lift to indecomposable 1-morphisms. Khovanov homology categorifies the Jones polynomial: $V_K(q)$ becomes the Euler characteristic of a bigraded chain complex $\mathrm{Kh}^{\bullet,\bullet}(K)$, and the latter is a strictly stronger invariant (it detects the unknot).

blocks.html:
See also
  • Category theory — monoidal categories
  • Representation theory — tensor and dual reps
</section>

Connections

Quantum groups sit at the intersection of three live frontiers: the quantum geometric Langlands program (Gaitsgory–Lurie) replaces the classical Langlands sheaf-on-$\mathrm{Bun}_G$ picture with categories of $U_q(\mathfrak g)$-modules and predicts a duality interchanging $q$ and the Langlands dual; 2-Kac–Moody algebras (Rouquier) categorify the entire Drinfeld–Jimbo machinery and link knot homology to derived categories of Lie-algebra representations; and quantum cluster algebras propose that canonical bases come from cluster monomials, but this is open in general type.

  • Lie algebras — root systems and Cartan classification — quantize root data, get a quantum group.
  • Representation theory — tensor and dual reps — the braided monoidal category $\mathrm{Rep}(U_q(\mathfrak g))$ is the deformation of $\mathrm{Rep}(G)$.
  • Category theory — monoidal categories — the natural home for braidings, ribbon structures, and quantum traces.
  • Algebraic topology — knot polynomials and their categorifications connect 3-manifold topology back to algebra.
  • Monstrous moonshine — vertex operator algebras and their quantum-group cousins govern modular characters; affine $U_q(\widehat{\mathfrak g})$ is closely related.
